schools.summary: Ellicott and Clarke fix Adullam's geography — a great cave in the Elah valley near the old Canaanite city, roomy enough for 400 refugees.
Henry reads the cave as a shining light eclipsed; David's tender care for his parents and his faith 'till I know what God will do for me.'
Gill notes David came glad of his deliverance from Achish at Gath.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott set the social scene: malcontents under Saul's misrule, whom David organised into a disciplined frontier force.
Clarke and Wesley defend the men's character — whatever they were before, David civilised them and held them to justice and honesty.
Gill glosses the distress as poverty, oppression, and afflictive providences.

debates.question: Were David's four hundred 'exceptionable characters' or merely the oppressed?

sides.position: oppressed and persecuted men whom David disciplined, not criminals
malcontents and bitter of soul, disaffected under Saul

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott place Mizpeh of Moab as a mountain fastness and ground the appeal in David's Moabite descent through Ruth.
Clarke sees prudent care for the parents beyond Saul's reach; Wesley hears in 'till I know' a faith wholly cast upon God.
Gill notes David felt safer in Moab, an enemy's land, than in Israel under Saul.

outliers.note: Observes that David reverently says 'Elohim' (God), not the covenant Name 'Jehovah,' when speaking to the idolatrous king of Moab.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott read the verse as a narrative hinge; 'the hold' is the same Moabite stronghold from which Gad bids David depart.
Wesley takes the singular 'hold' for the plural — the whole succession of refuges through which David was driven while his parents stayed safe in Moab.
Gill, with the Targum, notes David caused his parents to remain before the king of Moab by his leave.
Ellicott gives Gad a large place in David's later reign and reads his counsel as a divinely-prompted call back to Judah; he weighs 'forest' against 'city' of Hareth.
Wesley: David must show himself in Judah to claim the kingdom, and God thereby exercises and prepares his faith and courage.
Gill identifies Gad as one of the company of prophets under Samuel's presidency.

debates.question: Is the destination 'the forest' or 'the city' of Hareth?

sides.position: 'city' of Hareth — the LXX and Josephus reading, favoured by Conder, who finds no forest there
'forest' or 'thickets' — the Hebrew 'yar', still growing abundantly (Payne Smith)

schools.summary: Ellicott and Clarke reconstruct the scene — Saul under the tamarisk on the height in Gibeah, spear in hand, holding a council of war with his Benjamite captains.
Henry calls it a 'bloody court of inquisition' and anatomises jealous malice, which suspects treason where only friendship exists.
Gill: the news of David in arms filled Saul with fears and jealousies of all about him.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch expose Saul's favouritism — offices and lands hoarded for Benjamin, loyalty bought with fields and captaincies.
Wesley draws out the tribal appeal and its threat: David of Judah will never enrich Benjamin as Saul has done.
Gill: Saul seized the occasion of the report of David's forces to address his servants.
Ellicott finds the jealous spirit now mastering even Saul's own tribe and son; the pointed talk of a 'league' suggests he had learned of the New Moon covenant.
Clarke shows the charge impossible — none can prove a league that never existed; Wesley names it the wheedling art of jealousy.
Gill: Saul counted the servants' silence and unconcern as itself a conspiracy.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott identify Doeg as marshal or overseer of Saul's servants, whose eyewitness report (not hearsay) struck Saul hard.
Gill: Doeg, full of enmity and eager for preferment, outran the others and spoke first.
Clarke reckons Doeg the king's equerry or groom, set over the royal beasts.
Keil and Delitzsch accept that the inquiry happened (Ahimelech never denies it); Ellicott doubts it, thinking Doeg misled by the priest fetching the sword from behind the ephod.
Clarke thinks the consulting probable; Wesley stresses Doeg's lying and his suppression of David's deceit that exculpated the priest.
Gill weighs both readings and notes the Jewish view that Doeg framed it as treason, since Urim was consulted only for a king.

debates.question: Did Ahimelech really inquire of the Lord for David?

sides.position: yes — it happened, though unrecorded, and Ahimelech never denies it
doubtful — Doeg may have been misled by the priest going in to fetch the sword

schools.summary: Ellicott: summoning the whole priestly house, not Ahimelech alone, betrays a massacre already determined by Saul and Doeg.
Wesley: these are the priests of Eli's house, already under God's threatened sentence to be cut off.
Gill notes only that Saul sent messengers to summon Ahimelech before him.
Gill: Saul's 'thou son of Ahitub,' withholding name and priestly title, is a studied contempt for the second man in the kingdom.
Keil and Delitzsch quote indictment and defence together and find Ahimelech answering calmly and worthily, pleading David's proven faithfulness.
Ellicott: of the three counts, the inquiry of the Lord for David was the gravest in Saul's eyes — the point Ahimelech alone troubles to answer.
Gill: the word 'conspired' presumes the guilt it pretends to examine, turning an innocent kindness into sedition.
Ellicott hears the people's own estimate of David in the priest's dignified plea; Clarke suggests 'neeman' (faithful) may name a court office of trust.
Wesley: Ahimelech judges nothing of the present quarrel, only testifies to what David had been and still was for all he knew.
Gill frames the answer as spoken first for David, then for the priest himself.
Ellicott lays out three renderings and prefers Abarbanel's 'that was the first day'; the priest's defence rests on his believing David was on the king's errand.
Clarke: inquiring for David was nothing new and never forbidden; Ahimelech knew of no breach and treated him as the king's son-in-law.
Gill cites Kimchi's non-interrogative reading and voices the priest's innocence — had he known David fled, he would never have done it.

debates.question: How should 'Did I then begin to inquire of God for him?' be rendered?

sides.position: an indignant question implying long, customary practice of inquiring for David
a statement — 'that was the first day that I enquired' (Abarbanel, Kimchi)

schools.summary: Gill: Saul pronounces the death sentence himself, without counsel or delay, upon an innocent man — an act of arbitrary power and great injustice, reaching to the whole father's house.
Ellicott identifies the 'footmen' as the royal runners; Keil and Delitzsch note the repeated 'priests of the Lord' magnifies the horror of the order.
Clarke honours the guards for daring to disobey a command of such injustice and irreligion.
Gill records the tradition that the runners were Abner and Amasa, whom he takes for princes rather than mere footmen.

themes: loyalty to God above the king

schools.summary: Ellicott stresses the sacrilege of priests slain in their white robes; Clarke reconciles the numbers — 85 priests, 305/385 the whole population of Nob.
Clarke calls Doeg a ruthless Edomite; Wesley reads his foreign, priest-hating descent as why the deed fell to him and not to Israel.
Gill: none fitter for the butchery than the false accuser turned executioner.

debates.question: How many were slain — and how are the differing numbers reconciled?

sides.position: eighty-five priests (Hebrew), the ministers who wore the ephod
three hundred and five (LXX) or three hundred and eighty-five (Josephus) — the whole population of Nob

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott see Saul treating innocent Nob as a city under the ban, identifying private revenge with the cause of Jehovah; Ellicott links it to the Gibeonite bloodguilt of 2 Samuel 21.
Wesley: no wickedness in Saul's life compares to this; he is now wholly under the evil spirit's power.
Gill: Saul sent men to slay all the inhabitants of the city.

outliers.note: Conjectures that the unrecorded slaughter of the Gibeonites (2 Samuel 21:1) was this very destruction of Nob, where the Tabernacle's Gibeonite servants perished.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch translate David's confession of guilt and his pledge of protection; Ellicott traces Abiathar's whole career to his banishment under Solomon.
Henry reads Abiathar's flight to David typically — sufferers for the Son of David commit their souls to him — and notes David's grief and his penning of Psalm 52.
Gill: Abiathar carried the ephod with the Urim and Thummim in his escape, bringing the oracle to David.

themes: a priest for the coming king

schools.summary: Gill imagines the shocking disclosure — news David had perhaps not yet heard, a sorrowful account of the Lord's own priests slain.
Keil and Delitzsch set the report with its sequel, showing it draws from David at once both confession and the pledge 'Abide with me, fear not.'
Clarke: David, suspecting Doeg would inform, calls himself the 'innocent cause' of the priests' destruction — no wrong done, yet the guilt of the outcome owned.
Ellicott sets the confession against a long Talmudic enmity between David and Doeg, the old servant jealous for Saul's house.
Gill reads 'I knew it that day' as David's inward foreboding at Nob, now confirmed by the event.
Gill: David invites and encourages the frightened Abiathar to stay, bidding him fear neither Saul nor any other.
Clarke: 'he that seeketh my life seeketh thy life' — a common enemy makes common safety, and David is bound in conscience to defend him at his own risk.

themes: 'with me thou shalt be in safeguard'
